title: India at sea: The partner ASEAN can afford to trust
gist: India is becoming ASEAN's preferred maritime-security hedge against US-China rivalry — rising from ninth to sixth place in ISEAS's 2025 State of Southeast Asia survey — because it offers naval capability and disaster relief (Operations Sadbhav and Brahma) without territorial disputes or alignment demands, though its cooperation remains a patchwork of exercises and memoranda rather than standing architecture, and the bigger emerging risk is too many uncoordinated capacity-building initiatives from India, Australia, Japan, the US and the EU crowding the same areas.

themes.theme: India's rising maritime standing with ASEAN

insights.claim: India's strategic relevance to ASEAN rose from ninth place to sixth in a single year in the ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute's 2025 State of Southeast Asia survey, and it ranked among the region's preferred partners for hedging against US-China rivalry.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: A concrete, sourced ranking shift (9th to 6th) quantifying India's rising soft-power/security standing in Southeast Asia — a trackable metric for India's regional influence trajectory.
insights.locator: In the ISEAS–Yusof Ishak Institute's 2025 State of Southeast Asia survey

insights.claim: India's core value proposition to ASEAN is that it has one of the region's larger navies with reach to patrol and respond across Southeast Asian waters, yet has no territorial or maritime dispute with any Southeast Asian country — a distinction from China — meaning it can offer capability without forcing states to take sides between the US and China.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: Frames India's specific structural advantage (capability without alignment cost) versus both US and China as a maritime partner — a differentiator relevant to forecasting India's role in Indo-Pacific security architecture.
insights.locator: India's value is easy to overlook

themes.theme: Disaster relief as India's proof point

insights.claim: When Typhoon Yagi struck mainland Southeast Asia in September 2024, India launched Operation Sadbhav, sending relief to Vietnam, Laos and Myanmar within days.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: Concrete, dated evidence (Sept 2024, Typhoon Yagi) of India's rapid-response disaster diplomacy capability — a track record data point for assessing India's HADR (humanitarian assistance/disaster relief) credibility.
insights.locator: When Typhoon Yagi tore across mainland Southeast Asia

insights.claim: When a 7.7-magnitude earthquake struck Myanmar, India was among the first responders via Operation Brahma, deploying naval ships, air-force lift, a search-and-rescue team, and a field hospital that treated more than 2,500 patients.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: A second concrete, quantified (2,500 patients treated) HADR case reinforcing India's demonstrated rapid-response capacity — relevant for benchmarking India's disaster-diplomacy investment against US/China/Japan equivalents.
insights.locator: Six months later, when a 7.7-magnitude earthquake struck Myanmar

insights.claim: A neighbour that turns up fast and imposes no conditions is worth more to ASEAN than one that arrives with them — the core reason India's practical, low-conditionality crisis response has shifted regional perceptions toward viewing it as a provider of practical security rather than a claimant of regional leadership.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: A generalizable principle for middle-power regional influence-building: unconditional rapid disaster response builds more durable trust than formal security commitments — applicable as a benchmark for other powers courting ASEAN.
insights.quote: A neighbour that turns up fast and imposes no conditions is worth more than one that arrives with them.
insights.locator: That crisis capability rests on everyday maritime cooperation

insights.claim: In May 2023 India and Singapore co-hosted the inaugural ASEAN-India Maritime Exercise, the first time India exercised with the ASEAN grouping as a whole, building on years of coordinated patrols with Indonesia and Thailand against illegal fishing, trafficking and piracy, while India has worked through ASEAN's own institutions (co-chairing ADMM-Plus working groups) rather than building parallel structures.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: Shows India deliberately embedding itself in ASEAN-centric institutions rather than creating parallel frameworks — a structural signal of India's long-term regional-institution strategy versus a more unilateral US/China approach.
insights.locator: In May 2023 the Indian and Singapore navies co-hosted

insights.claim: India's comparative advantage in capacity-building without domination is one it shares with other ASEAN dialogue partners, particularly Australia, and extending India-Australia cooperation (already close in the eastern Indian Ocean and Bay of Bengal) into joint HADR planning, shared maritime domain awareness, and combined coast guard training in Southeast Asia would multiply the effect.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: A specific, actionable recommendation (India-Australia joint HADR/maritime-domain-awareness expansion into Southeast Asia) worth tracking as a concrete policy development to watch for.
insights.locator: Its comparative advantage, capacity-building without domination

insights.claim: The real risk ahead for ASEAN is not too little outside help but too much of it, poorly coordinated — India, Australia, Japan, the US and the EU are all running overlapping capacity-building initiatives in the same few areas (maritime domain awareness, HADR, cyber), risking duplicated effort and overwhelmed local institutions unless coordinated through ASEAN's own networks (ADMM-Plus, East Asia Summit, ARF).
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: A non-obvious risk flag: the binding constraint on ASEAN maritime security capacity-building is coordination overload among partners, not insufficient external investment — relevant for any organization designing a Southeast Asia security-assistance program.
insights.locator: The real risk ahead is not too little help

insights.claim: India's cooperation with ASEAN remains a patchwork of exercises and memoranda rather than a standing architecture, and follow-through is its key weakness, even as it is increasingly seen as a dependable crisis contributor and convener of others willing to do the same.
insights.speaker: Premesha Saha
insights.so_what: A balanced caveat against overstating India's institutionalization in Southeast Asia — useful check when assessing how durable/scalable India's regional security role actually is versus ad hoc.
insights.locator: None of this makes India a substitute for the security guarantees
